steps.prompt: Which words form the main verb of the first clause?

hints.flete: 'Flete' is a finite verb (imperative) but belongs to the second clause
hints.Filiae: 'Filiae' is a noun in the vocative — direct address

steps.target: to be unwilling / to not wish
choices: to be unwilling / to not wish

hints.to harm: 'To harm' is 'nocere' — 'nolite' comes from 'nolle' (to be unwilling)

steps.reveal: nolite flere = do not weep (negative imperative formed with 'nolo')

hints.indirect statement: Indirect statement uses accusative + infinitive — this is a direct command
hints.purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — this is a negative imperative
hints.result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — this is a prohibition using 'nolite'

hints.volo, velle, volui, —: These are the parts of 'volo' (to wish) — 'nolo' is the negative compound non + volo
hints.noceo, nocere, nocui, nocitum: These are the parts of 'noceo' (to harm)
hints.nosco, noscere, novi, notum: These are the parts of 'nosco' (to learn/know)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'fletis' — 'flete' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd plural would be 'fleatis' — 'flete' is imperative

hints.fluo, fluere, fluxi, fluxum: These are the parts of 'fluo' (to flow)
hints.flecto, flectere, flexi, flexum: These are the parts of 'flecto' (to bend)
hints.flagro, flagrare, flagravi, flagratum: These are the parts of 'flagro' (to burn/blaze)

steps.reveal: Filiae Hierusalem = Daughters of Jerusalem! (voc. pl.)

hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular 'filiae' looks the same, but context shows direct address
hints.nominative plural: Nominative plural 'filiae' looks the same, but this is a direct address (vocative)
hints.dative singular: Dative singular 'filiae' looks the same, but context is vocative address

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'filiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filiae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'filiae' ends in -ae

steps.reveal: Filiae Hierusalem = daughters of Jerusalem (Hierusalem is indeclinable)

hints.nominative: 'Hierusalem' is indeclinable — here it functions as genitive modifying 'Filiae'
hints.accusative: 'Hierusalem' is indeclinable — context shows it modifies 'Filiae' as a genitive
hints.ablative: 'Hierusalem' is indeclinable — there is no preposition; it is genitive of 'Filiae'

steps.reveal: super vos ipsas = over/for yourselves (acc. after 'super')

hints.nominative: Nominative 'vos' looks the same, but 'super' + acc. = over/for
hints.ablative: 'Super' can take ablative ('about'), but here with 'vos' it is accusative
hints.dative: Dative of 'vos' would be 'vobis' — 'vos' is accusative after 'super'

steps.prompt: What is 'ipsas' and what does it add?

hints.reflexive pronoun: Reflexive would be 'se' — 'ipsas' is intensive, adding emphasis to 'vos'
hints.relative pronoun: Relative would be 'quas' — 'ipsas' is an intensive pronoun
hints.demonstrative pronoun (those women): 'Ipsas' can function demonstratively but here it intensifies 'vos'

translation: Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves
